axes.label: Altruism
axes.description: Generous vs. Self-interested. Measures the default direction of resource flow — whether a subject feels a prosocial pull to distribute beyond themselves and kin, or whether obligation contracts tightly around personal and in-group welfare.

axes.label: Loyalty Scope
axes.description: Familial vs. Civic/Universal. Whether moral obligation radiates outward from concrete kinship and blood ties, or attaches preferentially to abstract collectives like the nation-state, the civic order, or humanity in general.

axes.label: Ethnic Ingroup Bias
axes.description: Ingroup-oriented vs. Cosmopolitan. Whether shared ethnicity, ancestry, and culture form a legitimate axis of political solidarity and trust, or whether belonging should be predominantly chosen and culture-blind.

axes.label: Time Preference
axes.description: High (Present/Consumption) vs. Low (Future/Capital Accumulation). Measures the willingness to sacrifice current utility for long-term or intergenerational structural stability — the subject's acceptable trade-off between current utility and future systemic fragility.

axes.label: Scope of Sovereignty
axes.description: Centralized Monolith vs. Distributed Edge Autonomy. Maps the preference for top-down, standardized enforcement versus local, self-hosted governance and subsidiarity.

axes.label: Resource Metaphysics
axes.description: Zero-Sum (Mercantilism/Scarcity) vs. Positive-Sum (Techno-capitalism/Abundance). Determines whether a subject views economic and social interactions strictly as extractive games or as generative networks.

axes.label: Risk Epistemology
axes.description: Precautionary Principle vs. Proactionary Principle. Measures systemic risk tolerance — whether to halt progress to prevent theoretical tail-risk catastrophes, or to accelerate development to engineer solutions to them.

axes.label: Normative Enforcement
axes.description: Guilt-driven vs. Shame-driven. Differentiates between internalized moral frameworks (objective right/wrong irrespective of external observation) and externalized social frameworks (reputation, honor, face).

axes.label: Authority Epistemology
axes.description: Heuristic (Tradition) vs. Technocratic (Rationalism). The preference for evolved, Chesterton's-fence social heuristics versus engineered, algorithmic optimization by domain experts.

axes.label: Ontological Focus
axes.description: Atomized vs. Institutional. Does the subject derive terminal value from individual self-actualization, or as a functional node operating within a larger historical or social apparatus?

questions.text: When I have more than I need, I feel a strong pull to share with people who have less.
It is reasonable to prioritize my own material gain over the welfare of strangers.
I should personally give more to charitable causes than I currently do.
My first obligations are to my family, even when they conflict with the wider community's interests.
I would accept personal sacrifice for the good of my country even if it hurt my family's prospects.
Ancestry and kinship matter more than abstract social contracts.
People generally have more in common with members of their own ethnic or cultural group.
Cultural and ethnic mixing enriches a society more than it threatens to erode it.
Shared ancestry is a legitimate basis for political solidarity.
Sacrificing a percentage of current economic growth to ensure the structural integrity of institutions a century from now is an acceptable, necessary trade-off.
Debt-leveraged consumption is a valid mechanism to maximize immediate utility, even if it introduces systemic tail-risk for future generations.
Intergenerational wealth transfers and legacy building hold higher terminal value than maximizing peak individual lifetime consumption.
The immediate alleviation of suffering supersedes the theoretical, long-term structural consequences of the interventions used to achieve it.
A society should prioritize massive capital expenditure on infrastructure with a 500-year payoff horizon over immediate consumer subsidies.
Complex systems are best managed by creating universal, standardized protocols enforced by a central monolithic authority.
Subsidiarity dictates that decision-making must be computationally localized to the smallest feasible node, even at the cost of global efficiency.
Secession and the deliberate forking of political entities are legitimate mechanisms for resolving irreconcilable social divergence.
Asymmetrical, planetary-scale threats (e.g. climate change, bio-terrorism) require the eventual consolidation of sovereignty into a single administrative apparatus.
Local failure modes (e.g. corruption in a small town) are preferable to the single point of failure inherent in a centralized bureaucracy.
Extreme wealth accumulation by individuals inherently extracts resources that would otherwise belong to the broader population.
Technological innovation systematically redefines scarcity, expanding the total carrying capacity of the system rather than merely reallocating fixed assets.
Geopolitics is fundamentally an entropic battle over fixed physical and energy resources, making perpetual peace impossible.
Capital allocation toward advanced computation and space exploration will eventually yield an economy untethered from terrestrial constraints.
In any transaction that generates extreme profit for one party, another party must necessarily be suffering an equivalent deficit.
Deployment of transformative technologies (e.g. artificial general intelligence, synthetic biology) must be legally paused until all tail-risk failure modes are provably bounded.
The optimal strategy for mitigating existential risk is accelerating technological capabilities to engineer countermeasures dynamically as threats emerge.
Systemic stagnation is a mathematically greater risk to human survival than the unintended externalities of rapid scientific advancement.
When a system's state space is sufficiently complex, the default stance toward altering its foundational parameters should be strict aversion.
Progress requires accepting that a certain statistical percentage of catastrophic failures is the unavoidable cost of evolutionary leaps.
An action alters an individual's moral state and requires correction regardless of whether the network ever discovers it.
Reputation and the preservation of social "face" are the most reliable, efficient mechanisms for maintaining coordination in a community.
Absolute philosophical principles hold greater weight than the contextual social friction caused by upholding them in public.
Ostracization and public disgrace are highly efficient, necessary protocols for correcting defective nodes in a social network.
I am more disturbed by the internal knowledge that I violated a core principle than I am by public humiliation for a harmless mistake.
Multi-generational traditions encode highly optimized survival heuristics that cannot be safely deduced from first principles by single generations.
Governance should be aggressively decoupled from popular sentiment and executed by specialized algorithms and domain experts.
Dismantling a historical social structure without fully understanding the exact selective pressures that built it is functionally suicidal.
Societal systems are inherently legible; they can be successfully modeled and optimized from the top down using rigorous statistical methods.
The "wisdom of the ancients" is mostly survivorship bias and holds little predictive power for managing modern, hyper-complex systems.
The absolute maximization of individual autonomy and self-actualization is the highest terminal value a society can achieve.
Meaning is entirely derived from one's functional utility within a broader, intergenerational structure (family, religion, or state).
Social obligations are strictly contractual; they can be terminated at will without inherent moral penalty if the ROI becomes negative.
The individual is a transient component of the species; preserving the overarching architecture of the civilization takes absolute precedence over personal liberty.
True freedom is not the absence of constraints, but the voluntary adoption of rigorous duties to a system larger than oneself.
